topic: Aspects of Political Life in the Hispanic World
subject: spanish

items.q: What is the gender of 'el día' (day)?
items.why: Students rely on '-a = feminine'. Common exceptions: el día, el mapa, el problema, el sistema, el clima (many Greek-origin -ma words). These appear constantly so must be memorised.

A notorious faux ami that causes real confusion. 'Estoy embarazada' = 'I am pregnant'. For 'embarrassed' use 'avergonzado/a'. Native speakers laugh at this one - learners avoid by association with shame.

items.q: How is 'll' typically pronounced in Spanish?
items.why: Students pronounce 'll' as English double L. In most Spanish dialects, ll is a YOD sound (like English y) or a 'zh' sound (Argentina). Never pronounced as a doubled English L.
Correct answer: Me gusta el español.. “Me gusto el español.” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which sentence means 'Maths is more difficult than English'?
items.correct: Las matemáticas son más difíciles que el inglés.
items.misconception: Las matemáticas es más difícil que el inglés.
items.random_wrong: Las matemáticas son menos difíciles que el inglés.
items.why: It's Las matemáticas son más difíciles que el inglés.. “Las matemáticas es más difícil que el inglés.” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many subject pronouns does Spanish have (yo, tú, él/ella/usted, nosotros, vosotros, ellos/ellas/ustedes)?
items.why: Exact answer: 6 pronouns. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
